metadata.rationale: The article's main medical takeaway is broadly accurate: suspected cardiac arrest requires calling 911, starting CPR, and using an AED if available rather than relying on ordinary transport. However, it slightly overstates the taxi warning into a near-universal rule for serious heart-related symptoms, and several accompanying graphics are edited translations redesigned to look like polished English public-health materials.

This is the first step as per instructions to use Wikipedia first.

best_trajectory.observation:
Warning Signs of a Heart Attack | American Heart Association: What are the warning signs of a heart attack? They include chest discomfort, arm, neck and jaw pain, shortness of breath, and more. (https://www.heart.org/en/health-topics/heart-attack/warning-signs-of-a-heart-attack)
Warning Signs of a Heart Attack | Circulation: Chest discomfort: Most heart attacks involve discomfort in the center of the chest that lasts for more than a few minutes or that goes away and comes back. The ... (https://www.ahajournals.org/doi/10.1161/CIRCULATIONAHA.113.006126)
Heart Attack, Stroke and Cardiac Arrest Symptoms: Symptoms can include pain or discomfort in one or both arms, the back, neck, jaw or stomach. SHORTNESS OF BREATH with or without chest discomfort. OTHER SIGNS (https://www.heart.org/en/about-us/heart-attack-and-stroke-symptoms)
[PDF] Warning Signs of Heart Attack: Other signs: These may include breaking out in a cold sweat, nausea or lightheadedness. As with men, women's most common heart attack symptom is chest pain or ... (https://www.heart.org/en/-/media/Files/Health-Topics/Answers-by-Heart/What-Are-the-Warning-Signs-of-Heart-Attack.pdf)
Understand Your Risks to Prevent a Heart Attack: High blood pressure; High cholesterol; Diabetes; Overweight or obesity; Family history of early atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease ... (https://www.heart.org/en/health-topics/heart-attack/understand-your-risks-to-prevent-a-heart-attack)

best_trajectory.observation:
The image appears to be a designed infographic/illustration rather than a real photograph, so “manipulation” mainly means graphic editing/layout.

**Signs of editing**
- The two people’s faces are covered with rectangular blurred blocks. These look like intentional pixelation/blur overlays (privacy redaction), not natural motion blur.
- Flat, vector-style drawings with clean outlines, uniform colors, and icon elements (heart/ECG line, sweat drops) indicate it’s digitally created artwork.

**Signs it’s not AI-synthetic or deceptively composited**
- Text is clean and readable with consistent fonts and alignment; no typical AI text warping or misspellings.
- Lines, shadows, and spacing are consistent across elements (title banner, numbered symptom boxes), suggesting standard graphic design software rather than an AI-generated scene.
- No obvious cut-and-paste seams, mismatched lighting, or perspective conflicts (it’s a flat layout).

**Counterfactual elements**
- None visible in the image itself beyond being a simplified educational graphic. The blur blocks obscure faces, but that’s consistent with deliberate redaction rather than fabrication.
